task_description: Clarify requirements for turning the existing v1 multi-agent demo into real agent usage with a head node that partitions work, assigns subtasks, and uses a context-engineered task packet.
initial_idea: now I finish v1's contain and I want to keep building real agent usage and division of works. I only has a rough idea: 1. user assign task to head node (and all commuticate and report should be respond by head node) 2. head node's task: base on agent and their desc in current env, partition the task, task pipeline, and assign to correspond agent. 3. each assigned subtask should base on best pratice of context engineer: Goal, Context, Constraints, Done when, Next.

rounds.question: For the next version, which milestone do you want first? A. real execution now: the head node actually invokes agents and waits for real outputs B. contract first: the head node produces the new task packet and pipeline, but execution can stay simulated for one more version C. both together in one pass Answer with A, B, or C, and add one proof signal for “this is real enough” from your perspective.

For this contract-first milestone, what must the head node produce before any real agent execution exists yet? Answer in this shape: must have: ... not in this milestone: ... Use concrete items like: structured plan, per-agent task packet, explicit handoff chain, head-only user summary, approval gate rules, retry/failure contract, agent discovery from current env, agent-to-agent direct messaging, real subagent invocation
rounds.answer: must have: structured plan, per-agent task packet, explicit handoff chain, head-only user summary, agent discovery from current env, agent-to-agent direct messaging

rounds.question: Boundary question: when you say `agent-to-agent direct messaging`, which rule do you want? A. agents may message each other directly, but only the head node may talk to the user B. agents may not message each other directly; every handoff/report must go through head C. agents may message each other directly only after the head has explicitly authorized that edge in the plan Answer with `A`, `B`, or `C`, and one sentence on why.

If agents can message each other directly, what must the head node still receive? A. every direct message in full B. only summarized checkpoints C. only final outputs plus explicit blockers D. the head must explicitly approve each new peer-to-peer edge before it is used Answer with `A`, `B`, `C`, or `D`, and add one short reason.
rounds.answer: typically C. And If it plan the workflow include return result or intermedia from some node.

rounds.question: What should this milestone explicitly not do yet? Answer in this shape: `not in this milestone: ...` Pick 2-4 concrete exclusions, for example: real subagent invocation, auto-retry / failure recovery policy, token or cost budgeting, approval gate system redesign, visual graph redesign, persistence of per-agent private memory, ranking/scoring agents by performance
rounds.answer: not in this milestone: real subagent invocation, auto-retry / failure recovery policy, token or cost budgeting, approval gate system redesign and the testing model using local codex (could run from tmux? )

rounds.question: What are the 2 or 3 concrete proof signals that make this contract-first milestone done? Answer in this shape: `done when: ...` Include whether local Codex via `tmux` is: required now, nice to have later, or out of scope for this milestone Examples of proof signals: submitting one task produces a structured plan plus per-agent packets in the run data, the head summary is derived from those packets, not hardcoded text, the UI can show which nodes must return intermediate results, one test proves agent discovery and packet generation from current env works
rounds.answer: 1. Codex via tmux required now. 2. submitting one task produces a structured plan, pipeline of workflow, per-agent's task. and this could implement by some prompt or agent skill on head node. 3. fix format for task assign base on best pratice of context engineer. (you could refine/modify it to fix our goal) 4. each subtask could view their task input (will be 3. output)

rounds.question: Pressure pass: your latest answer conflicts with an earlier boundary. Earlier: not in this milestone: `real subagent invocation` Now: `Codex via tmux required now` Which interpretation is correct? A. only the head node may run local Codex via `tmux` now, to generate the plan and per-agent task packets; role agents still do not execute for real B. the head node and role agents should all run for real via local Codex/`tmux` now C. no live Codex execution is needed inside the app yet; `tmux` is only for manual developer testing outside the product flow
rounds.answer: A. and current subagent could only show assigned task for poc.

rounds.question: In this app, what should `agent discovery from current env` actually mean for the head node? A. only the agents currently placed/selected in the canvas for this session B. all registered agents known by the app, even if not placed on the canvas yet C. a separate runtime source outside the current UI state

codebase_context.repo_shape: existing React/TypeScript app with event-projected multi-agent demo
evidence: src/shared/contracts/types.ts already models head vs role agents
src/server/orchestrator/scenario.ts is currently synthetic and replay-based
src/hooks/use-live-drain.ts drains queued events instead of invoking real agents

summary.milestone_type: Contract-first, with live head-node Codex via tmux only.
must_have: Agent discovery from selected canvas agents
summary.execution_boundary: Only the head node runs local Codex via tmux now; subagents only show assigned tasks for PoC.
summary.visibility_rule: Head receives final outputs and blockers by default, with plan-defined intermediate-return exceptions.
summary.agent_discovery: Only current session canvas-selected agents are discoverable.
